titleStmt.title: Letter from Philip C. MacDermot to Henry Arthur Wynne, 27 July 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Philip C. MacDermot

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from Philip C MacDermot (b. 1863), the Office of the Crown Solicitor in Co. Roscommon to Henry Arthur Wynne (1867-1945), the Chief Crown Solicitor, Dublin Castle asking for a refund of 11 shillings and 6 pence used for employing a typist to type warrants.Henry Arthur Wynne was the Chief Crown Solicitor for Ireland following the death of Malachy Kelly (1850-1916).

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
OFFICE OF CROWN SOLICITOR FOR CO. ROSCOMMON, BOYLE 27th Jul 1916 Dear Sir With reference to the cases under the 'Crimes Act' about
which I had an interview with you on Tuesday, I found it absolutely
necessary yesterday at Roscommon to employ a Typewriter to type the
Warrants which, you are aware, are very long as you advised me to set
out the full charge as drafted by you in the Summonses, and so as to
get the matter completed yesterday (and this was not done until eight
o'clock p.m.) I had, with the District Inspector, to find a Typewriter
and agree to pay him 11/6. So will you kindly let me know if you can
arrange for me to get a refund of this? As you are aware the matter
was urgent, and if all these warrants had to be written out, or if I
was not able to get assistance at Roscommon yesterday, they could not
have been completed in time, as it was, the Resident Magistrate had to
wait until eight o'clock. The Police witnesses, who had to make the
informations, were unable to arrive at Roscommon until the one o'clock
train. Yours truly Philip C MacDermot The Chief Crown Solicitor Dublin Castle Attorney General
In the circumstances I think it would
be reasonable and fair to allow Mr
MacDermot the 11/6 paid Typewriter
H.A.W.
29.vii.16 Let the sum asked for
be paid Authorise Crown Solicitor to
pay. Enclose copy of this sheet.
(the typewriters receipt to be furnished)
